rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe but common acute allergic contact dermatitis (plant/Rhus-induced) after garden brush exposure. The patient is systemically well and afebrile with the source's own red-flag screen negative (no facial/airway swelling, no mucosal involvement, no skin detachment, no secondary-infection features; Risk Level: Low), so this is neither an emergency (not T4/T5) nor strictly an urgent same-day problem (not T3). It is telehealth/GP-manageable with cool compresses, urushiol decontamination, oral antihistamine and potent topical corticosteroid. Because the severe blistering warrants a short course of prescription-only oral corticosteroid, AND telehealth cannot fully examine the skin/mucosae to exclude the blistering mimics, the disposition is PROMPT review within 24-48 hours (T2) rather than pure self-care (T0/T1) — with prominent mandatory escalation edges for anaphylaxis/airway, SJS/TEN and secondary infection. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED here is over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Swelling of the face, lips, tongue or throat, difficulty breathing or swallowing, widespread hives, or feeling faint
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of an evolving systemic allergic reaction / anaphylaxis or airway angioedema — a life-threatening emergency needing adrenaline and an ambulance. Breath-Ezy cannot give IM adrenaline, so the correct action is immediate 000.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your face, lips or throat start to swell, you have any trouble breathing or swallowing, break out in hives all over, or feel faint, call 000 straight away — this can be a serious allergic reaction.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sores or ulcers in the mouth, eyes or genitals, painful skin peeling or detaching beyond the exposed areas, or feeling generally unwell with the rash
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department the same day (call 000 if rapidly unwell)
escalation_edges.rationale: Mucosal involvement or skin detachment with systemic upset suggests a severe mucocutaneous reaction (Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / TEN) — a dermatological emergency with high mortality needing urgent hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sores in your mouth, eyes or genitals, or the skin starts peeling off in other areas, or you feel unwell in yourself, go to an emergency department the same day — this needs to be checked urgently.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blister fluid turning thick, yellow or foul-smelling, spreading redness and heat beyond the affected skin, or a fever/rigors
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person review (emergency department if it can't be arranged quickly)
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates a secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis of the ruptured excoriated bullae that needs antibiotics and in-person assessment — a complication telehealth cannot palpate for.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the fluid from the blisters turns thick, yellow or smelly, the redness spreads and the skin gets hot, or you develop a fever or the shivers, get seen in person the same day for a possible skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline: severe but systemically-well plant allergic contact dermatitis with a negative red-flag screen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start cool compresses, decontamination, antihistamine and topical steroid now, and arrange prompt review (in person or telehealth) within 24-48 hours to obtain the short oral corticosteroid course
escalation_edges.rationale: The proportionate disposition once emergencies are excluded — begin patient-performable treatment immediately and be reviewed promptly because the severe blistering warrants a prescription-only oral steroid and the skin/mucosae cannot be fully examined over telehealth.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Start cool compresses, wash off the plant oil and use an antihistamine now, and arrange to be reviewed within a day or two so a clinician can look at the skin and start a short course of steroid tablets for the severe blistering.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No improvement after 3-5 days of treatment, or worsening/spreading despite it
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Prompt GP review within 24-48 hours if not settling as expected
escalation_edges.rationale: A contact dermatitis that fails to settle on treatment may be misdiagnosed, may have a secondary infection, or may need reassessment and a different approach.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it hasn't started improving within 3-5 days, or it's getting worse or spreading despite the treatment, book a prompt GP review to reassess.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Severe sleep deprivation and blistered, swollen hands limiting manual dexterity
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Check the patient can practically carry out self-care (compresses, decontamination, applying cream) and reach prompt review despite impaired hand function and exhaustion; arrange support or lower the threshold to be seen in person if they cannot manage.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a common allergic skin reaction to a plant you contacted while gardening — distressing and blistering, but not an emergency. Right now: apply cool damp compresses instead of scratching, do NOT pop the blisters, and wash your skin, clothes, gloves and tools with soapy water to remove any leftover plant oil. Take an antihistamine for the itch and apply the steroid ointment. Because the blistering is severe, arrange to be reviewed within a day or two (in person or by a telehealth prescriber) to confirm it and get a short course of steroid tablets. You do not need an emergency department for the rash itself.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange prompt review within 24-48 hours (own GP, an after-hours GP, or a telehealth prescriber for the oral steroid); 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) is available for advice
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If this is after hours you do not need an emergency department for the rash itself — start the compresses, decontamination and antihistamine and arrange review in the next day or two. Only seek same-day/emergency care if a red flag develops: facial/lip/throat swelling or breathing difficulty (call 000), mouth/eye/genital sores or spreading skin peeling (same-day ED), or thick foul pus with spreading redness and fever (same-day review).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Tell the reviewing clinician the rash came up ~24 hours ago, about 12 hours after clearing brush/weeds in the garden, in linear streaks on both forearms/hands, with vesicles and bullae and no mucosal, airway or systemic features; note there were no new medications and no known allergies.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the blistering represents a benign plant contact dermatitis or one of the dangerous mimics (SJS/TEN, evolving anaphylaxis, or early secondary infection) — telehealth cannot fully examine the skin and mucosae.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Actively screen for mucosal involvement, skin detachment, airway/facial swelling and infection; treat a negative screen plus classic exposure geometry as a reason for prompt review and explicit safety-netting while starting patient-performable measures now — NOT as a reason to default to 000/ED when the patient is systemically well.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either dismissing a severe blistering eruption with pure self-care and no review or safety-net (under-action), or escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen straight to the emergency department / 000 (over-escalation).

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing a severe blistering eruption as trivial with pure self-care and no clinician review and no safety-net for anaphylaxis, SJS/TEN or secondary infection.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unrecognised SJS/TEN or evolving anaphylaxis progresses without anyone examining the skin/mucosae or advising when to escalate, or a secondary infection is missed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising only a routine (1-2 week) appointment with no mimic/infection safety-net, delaying treatment of a severe blistering dermatitis that warrants prompt review and a short steroid course.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen to the emergency department or calling 000 for a common plant allergic contact dermatitis — unnecessary ED load and patient distress/cost when prompt review is proportionate (same-day T3 review is an acceptable more-cautious disposition, not scored as over-triage).
